codeReview.summary: AutoFetchController @Observable @MainActor cleanly implements sleep-first-fetch-second semantics. retarget(to:) cancels any prior task before spawning a new one, early-returns for nil focus or toggle-off, and the loop re-checks both the toggle and currentFocusID on every tick so an off-flip or focus-swap during the sleep window silently skips the fetch rather than firing on stale state. stop() is idempotent (nil-safe cancel + clear currentFocusID). intervalSeconds is @ObservationIgnored and var so tests override with 1s without triggering view redraws. RootView wires: .task(id: repoStore.focus?.repo.id) → retarget, .onChange(of: autoFetchEnabled) → retarget, .onDisappear → stop. 6 ViewModel tests using real bare-upstream fixtures (1s interval) indirectly observe 'did a fetch fire?' via `git rev-parse origin/main` — elegant test pattern that avoids test-only instrumentation in production code. VAL-NET-007 strict (non-focused repos never fetched) is proven by the cancelsOnFocusChange test which pushes upstream commits to focus A, swaps to focus B before the first tick, waits past the interval, and asserts A's origin/main has NOT moved.

issues.description: Captured `focus` is strong-held across the Task closure for the lifetime of the timer. RepoFocus is class-backed so this is a retain cycle as long as the Task runs; cancel on retarget + stop handle teardown correctly today. But a RepoFocus whose timer retargets to nil (focus cleared) relies on the cancel + task=nil to release — verify this is invariant. The guard at line 97 (focus.repo.id == currentFocusID) stops the loop on stale focus but doesn't nil the captured `focus` — the closure still holds it until the containing Task completes. Non-blocking; documenting here so M6 doesn't copy the pattern with a longer-lived focus.

Task.sleep on `interval * 1_000_000_000` as UInt64 — at default 300s × 1e9 = 3e11, comfortably under UInt64.max. But a test setting intervalSeconds = UInt64.max would overflow the multiplication silently. Practically impossible today; consider clamping intervalSeconds to a sane upper bound in the setter if intervalSeconds ever becomes user-configurable.

.onDisappear calls autoFetch.stop(). For a multi-window setup (⌘N — VAL-UI-009), closing the second window will stop() the shared singleton controller, disabling auto-fetch for the remaining window. Not in M5 scope but worth flagging: the controller is singleton at the app level (one per KiteApp), so per-window lifecycle is tricky. M8's multi-window story will need to either make AutoFetchController per-window or scope stop() to a reference-count.

`_ = await ops.fetch(on: focus)` ignores the Bool result. Fine for auto-fetch UX (the toast surface already reports outcome) but means a stream of auth failures produces a stream of sticky red toasts on every interval until the user fixes auth or toggles off. Consider a 'suppress further auto-fetch errors after 3 consecutive failures' policy. Not in M5 scope; note for M8 polish.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Sleep-first-fetch-second is the correct UX — selecting a repo doesn't trigger a barrage. Re-reading autoFetchEnabled on each tick (not just at retarget) means the toggle propagates without requiring the user to change focus. currentFocusID guard closes the 'retarget mid-sleep' race.

M5-auto-fetch introduces NO new per-focus observer. AutoFetchController piggybacks on the existing RepoStore.focus via .task(id:) — no .onChange(of: focus.lastChangeAt) added. AGENTS.md consolidation guidance stands; M7 will be the critical one.

AutoFetchControllerTests observe 'did a fetch fire?' via `git rev-parse origin/main` on the tracker fixture rather than instrumenting NetworkOps for test-only counters. This keeps production code clean and proves end-to-end timer → NetworkOps → Git.stream → fetch → origin ref advancement.
